text:
Self-attention has long been used in natural language processing (NLP) for modeling relationships between sequences of words. Recently self-attention has become a hot topic in computer vision (CV) as well. However, there are three major obstacles for applying self-attention to images. First, most images are 2D data and treating them as 1D sequences neglects important spatial information. Second, the quadratic complexity with respect to the length of the input sequence (i.e., the spatial size of images) is expensive, especially for high-resolution images. Third, while the standard self-attention model can exploit spatial features, it cannot adapt to channels of different receptive fields, which is important for deep networks. In this work, we propose Large Kernel Attention (LKA) — a linear attention model that can retain self-adaptive long-range correlations while alleviating the drawbacks of self-attention. By factoring large-kernel convolutions, LKA benefits from large receptive field and multi-dimensional adaptability in an efficient way.

This module is developed into the Visual Attention Network (VAN). Surprisingly, though the architecture is very simple, it achieves the state-of-the-art performance on a large number of downstream tasks, such as image classification, object detection, segmentation and pose estimation, etc. And the performance of VAN is always better than those of state-of-the-art CNNs and Vision Transformers (ViTs) with similar model scale. e.g. the model VAN-B6 achieves 87.8% on ImageNet-1K which is the new state-of-the-art on panoptic segmentation with 58.2 PQ, and also achieves 50.1 mIoU on ADE20K which is 4 points higher than Swin-T, and 48.8 AP on COCO which is 2.6 points higher than Swin-T. The Visual Attention Network is a strong, simple and universal baseline for computer vision. Our code and models are available at: https://github.com/Visual-Attention-Network.
explanation: Text has precise, domain-specific metrics and a direct project URL, which is typical of a real research paper rather than generic AI-generated text.
